subject: Science
description: Communicate the logical connection among hypotheses, science concepts, tests conducted, data collected, and conclusions drawn from the scientific evidence.
description_plain: Your child can explain how their guesses, tests, data, and conclusions connect in a science investigation.

subject: Science
description: Use a variety of print and electronic resources (including the World Wide Web) to collect information and evidence as part of a research project.
description_plain: Your child can use books, websites, and other resources to find information for a science project.

subject: Science
description: Select and use appropriate tools and technology (including calculators, computers, balances, spring scales, microscopes, and binoculars) to perform tests, collect data, and display data.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use tools like calculators, microscopes, and scales to do tests and collect data.

subject: Science
description: Investigation and Experimentation: Scientific progress is made by asking meaningful questions and conducting careful investigations. As a basis for understanding this concept and addressing the content in the other three strands, students should develop their own questions and perform investigations. Students will:
description_plain: Your child learns that asking good questions and doing careful experiments helps us learn new science.

subject: Science
description: Students know that contractions of the heart generate blood pressure and that heart valves prevent backflow of blood in the circulatory system.
description_plain: Your child understands that the heart’s contractions create blood pressure and heart valves keep blood flowing the right way.

subject: Science
description: Students know how levers confer mechanical advantage and how the application of this principle applies to the musculoskeletal system.
description_plain: Your child learns how levers help us do work and how this idea applies to our muscles and bones.

subject: Science
description: Students know how to compare joints in the body (wrist, shoulder, thigh) with structures used in machines and simple devices (hinge, ball-and-socket, and sliding joints).
description_plain: Your child can compare joints in the body to parts in machines, like hinges and ball-and-socket joints.

subject: Science
description: Students know the angle of reflection of a light beam is equal to the angle of incidence.
description_plain: Your child knows that when light bounces off a surface, the angle it hits equals the angle it reflects.

subject: Science
description: Students know light can be reflected, refracted, transmitted, and absorbed by matter.
description_plain: Your child understands that light can bounce off, bend, pass through, or be absorbed by different materials.

subject: Science
description: Students know that white light is a mixture of many wavelengths (colors) and that retinal cells react differently to different wavelengths.
description_plain: Your child knows that white light is made of many colors and that our eyes react differently to each color.

subject: Science
description: Students know how simple lenses are used in a magnifying glass, the eye, a camera, a telescope, and a microscope.
description_plain: Your child learns how simple lenses are used in things like magnifying glasses, eyes, cameras, telescopes, and microscopes.

subject: Science
description: Students know light travels in straight lines if the medium it travels through does not change.
description_plain: Your child understands that light travels straight unless it passes through something that changes its path.

subject: Science
description: Students know that for an object to be seen, light emitted by or scattered from it must be detected by the eye.
description_plain: Your child knows that to see an object, light from or reflected by it must reach our eyes.

subject: Science
description: Students know visible light is a small band within a very broad electromagnetic spectrum.
description_plain: Your child understands that visible light is just a small part of a much larger range of electromagnetic waves.

subject: Science
description: Physical Principles in Living Systems (Physical Sciences): Physical principles underlie biological structures and functions. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child learns that physical science ideas help explain how living things are built and how they work.

subject: Science
description: Students know how to relate the structures of the eye and ear to their functions.
description_plain: Your child knows how the parts of the eye and ear help us see and hear.

subject: Science
description: Students know the structures and processes by which flowering plants generate pollen, ovules, seeds, and fruit.
description_plain: Your child learns how flowering plants make pollen, eggs, seeds, and fruit.

subject: Science
description: Students know the function of the umbilicus and placenta during pregnancy.
description_plain: Your child understands the roles of the umbilical cord and placenta during pregnancy.

subject: Science
description: Students know how the reproductive organs of the human female and male generate eggs and sperm and how sexual activity may lead to fertilization and pregnancy.
description_plain: Your child knows how male and female reproductive organs make sperm and eggs and how fertilization can lead to pregnancy.

subject: Science
description: Students know how bones and muscles work together to provide a structural framework for movement.
description_plain: Your child learns how bones and muscles work together to help the body move.

subject: Science
description: Students know organ systems function because of the contributions of individual organs, tissues, and cells. The failure of any part can affect the entire system.
description_plain: Your child understands that organs, tissues, and cells all work together in systems, and if one part fails, it can affect the whole system.

subject: Science
description: Students know plants and animals have levels of organization for structure and function, including cells, tissues, organs, organ systems, and the whole organism.
description_plain: Your child knows that plants and animals have different levels of organization, like cells, tissues, organs, and systems.

subject: Science
description: Structure and Function in Living Systems: The anatomy and physiology of plants and animals illustrate the complementary nature of structure and function. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child learns that the way plants and animals are built helps them do their jobs well.

subject: Science
description: Students know how to explain significant developments and extinctions of plant and animal life on the geologic time scale
description_plain: Your child can explain important changes and extinctions of plants and animals over Earth’s history.

subject: Science
description: Students know how movements of Earth’s continental and oceanic plates through time, with associated changes in climate and geographic connections, have affected the past and present distribution of organisms.
description_plain: Your child understands how the movement of Earth’s plates and changes in climate have affected where plants and animals live.

subject: Science
description: Students know fossils provide evidence of how life and environmental conditions have changed.
description_plain: Your child knows that fossils show how life and environments have changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Students know that evidence from geologic layers and radioactive dating indicates Earth is approximately 4.6 billion years old and that life on this planet has existed for more than 3 billion years.
description_plain: Your child understands that Earth is about 4.6 billion years old and that life has existed for over 3 billion years.

subject: Science
description: Students know that the rock cycle includes the formation of new sediment and rocks and that rocks are often found in layers, with the oldest generally on the bottom
description_plain: Your child learns about the rock cycle and that rocks are often found in layers, with the oldest layers at the bottom.

subject: Science
description: Students know the history of life on Earth has been disrupted by major catastrophic events, such as major volcanic eruptions or the impacts of asteroids.
description_plain: Your child knows that big events like volcanic eruptions or asteroid impacts have caused major changes in life on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Students know Earth processes today are similar to those that occurred in the past and slow geologic processes have large cumulative effects over long periods of time.
description_plain: Your child understands that the Earth changes today in ways similar to the past, and slow changes can add up to big effects over a long time.

subject: Science
description: Earth and Life History (Earth Sciences): Evidence from rocks allows us to understand the evolution of life on Earth. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that by studying rocks, we can learn how life on Earth has changed over time.

subject: Science
description: Students know that extinction of a species occurs when the environment changes and the adaptive characteristics of a species are insufficient for its survival.
description_plain: Your child knows that species can die out when their environment changes and they cannot adapt well enough to survive.

subject: Science
description: Students know how to construct a simple branching diagram to classify living groups of organisms by shared derived characteristics and how to expand the diagram to include fossil organisms.
description_plain: Your child can make a simple family tree to group living things by shared traits and add fossils to the tree.

subject: Science
description: Students know how independent lines of evidence from geology, fossils, and comparative anatomy provide the bases for the theory of evolution.
description_plain: Your child understands that evidence from rocks, fossils, and body structures helps explain how species have evolved.

subject: Science
description: Students know the reasoning used by Charles Darwin in reaching his conclusion that natural selection is the mechanism of evolution.
description_plain: Your child knows how Charles Darwin used observations to figure out that natural selection causes evolution.

subject: Science
description: Students know both genetic variation and environmental factors are causes of evolution and diversity of organisms.
description_plain: Your child understands that changes in genes and the environment cause species to evolve and become diverse.

subject: Science
description: Evolution: Biological evolution accounts for the diversity of species developed through gradual processes over many generations. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that evolution explains how many different species have developed slowly over many generations.

subject: Science
description: Students know DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the genetic material of living organisms and is located in the chromosomes of each cell.
description_plain: Your child knows that DNA is the genetic material found in the chromosomes inside every cell.

subject: Science
description: Students know plant and animal cells contain many thousands of different genes and typically have two copies of every gene. The two copies (or alleles) of the gene may or may not be identical, and one may be dominant in determining the phenotype while the other is recessive.
description_plain: Your child understands that plant and animal cells have many genes, usually two copies of each, which can affect traits differently.

subject: Science
description: Students know an inherited trait can be determined by one or more genes.
description_plain: Your child knows that traits passed down from parents come from one or more genes.

subject: Science
description: Students know sexual reproduction produces offspring that inherit half their genes from each parent.
description_plain: Your child understands that offspring get half their genes from each parent through sexual reproduction.

subject: Science
description: Students know the differences between the life cycles and reproduction methods of sexual and asexual organisms.
description_plain: Your child knows the difference between how organisms reproduce sexually and asexually and their life cycles.

subject: Science
description: Genetics: A typical cell of any organism contains genetic instructions that specify its traits. Those traits may be modified by environmental influences. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that cells have genetic instructions for traits, which can be changed by the environment.

subject: Science
description: Students know that as multicellular organisms develop, their cells differentiate.
description_plain: Your child knows that as multicellular organisms grow, their cells become specialized for different jobs.

subject: Science
description: Students know cells divide to increase their numbers through a process of mitosis, which results in two daughter cells with identical sets of chromosomes.
description_plain: Your child understands that cells divide by mitosis to make two identical cells with the same chromosomes.

subject: Science
description: Students know that mitochondria liberate energy for the work that cells do and that chloroplasts capture sunlight energy for photosynthesis.
description_plain: Your child knows that mitochondria provide energy for cells and chloroplasts use sunlight to make food in plants.

subject: Science
description: Students know the nucleus is the repository for genetic information in plant and animal cells.
description_plain: Your child understands that the nucleus holds the genetic information in plant and animal cells.

subject: Science
description: Students know the characteristics that distinguish plant cells from animal cells, including chloroplasts and cell walls.
description_plain: Your child knows that plant cells have chloroplasts and cell walls, which animal cells do not have.

subject: Science
description: Students know cells function similarly in all living organisms.
description_plain: Your child understands that cells work in similar ways in all living things.

subject: Science
description: Cell Biology: All living organisms are composed of cells, from just one to many trillions, whose details usually are visible only through a microscope. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that all living things are made of cells, which are usually too small to see without a microscope.

subject: Science
description: Identify changes in natural phenomena over time without manipulating the phenomena (e.g., a tree limb, a grove of trees, a stream, a hillslope).
description_plain: Your child can notice changes in natural things like trees or streams over time without changing them.

subject: Science
description: Interpret events by sequence and time from natural phenomena (e.g., the relative ages of rocks and intrusions).
description_plain: Your child can figure out the order and timing of natural events, like how old rocks are compared to others.

subject: Science
description: Read a topographic map and a geologic map for evidence provided on the maps and construct and interpret a simple scale map.
description_plain: Your child can read maps that show land shapes and rock types and make simple maps using scale.

subject: Science
description: Recognize whether evidence is consistent with a proposed explanation.
description_plain: Your child can tell if evidence supports a given explanation.

subject: Science
description: Communicate the steps and results from an investigation in written reports and oral presentations.
description_plain: Your child can explain what they did and found in an investigation by writing or speaking.

subject: Science
description: Construct appropriate graphs from data and develop qualitative statements about the relationships between variables.
description_plain: Your child can make graphs from data and describe how different things are related.

subject: Science
description: Select and use appropriate tools and technology (including calculators, computers, balances, spring scales, microscopes, and binoculars) to perform tests, collect data, and display data.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use tools like calculators, microscopes, and scales to do tests and collect data.

subject: Science
description: Develop a hypothesis.
description_plain: Develop a hypothesis.

subject: Science
description: Students know the natural origin of the materials used to make common objects.
description_plain: Your child knows that materials used to make everyday objects come from natural sources.

subject: Science
description: Students know different natural energy and material resources, including air, soil, rocks, minerals, petroleum, fresh water, wildlife, and forests, and know how to classify them as renewable or nonrenewable.
description_plain: Your child understands different natural resources like air, water, and minerals and can tell which ones can be replaced and which cannot.

subject: Science
description: Students know the utility of energy sources is determined by factors that are involved in converting these sources to useful forms and the consequences of the conversion process.
description_plain: Your child knows that how useful an energy source is depends on how it is changed into usable energy and the effects of that process.

subject: Science
description: Resources: Sources of energy and materials differ in amounts, distribution, usefulness, and the time required for their formation. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that energy and material sources vary in amount, location, usefulness, and how long they take to form.

subject: Science
description: Students know the number and types of organisms an ecosystem can support depends on the resources available and on abiotic factors, such as quantities of light and water, a range of temperatures, and soil composition.
description_plain: Your child knows that the number and types of organisms in an ecosystem depend on available resources and non-living factors like light, water, temperature, and soil.

subject: Science
description: Students know different kinds of organisms may play similar ecological roles in similar biomes.
description_plain: Your child understands that different organisms can have similar roles in similar environments.

subject: Science
description: Students know populations of organisms can be categorized by the functions they serve in an ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child knows that groups of organisms can be sorted by the jobs they do in an ecosystem.

subject: Science
description: Students know matter is transferred over time from one organism to others in the food web and between organisms and the physical environment.
description_plain: Your child understands that matter moves over time between organisms and their environment through food webs.

subject: Science
description: Students know energy entering ecosystems as sunlight is transferred by producers into chemical energy through photosynthesis and then from organism to organism through food webs.
description_plain: Your child knows that energy from sunlight is changed into food energy by plants and then passed along through food webs.

subject: Science
description: Ecology: Organisms in ecosystems exchange energy and nutrients among themselves and with the environment. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that organisms in ecosystems share energy and nutrients with each other and their surroundings.

subject: Science
description: Students know differences in pressure, heat, air movement, and humidity result in changes of weather.
description_plain: Your child knows that changes in pressure, heat, wind, and humidity cause different weather.

subject: Science
description: Students know convection currents distribute heat in the atmosphere and oceans.
description_plain: Your child understands that heat moves around the air and oceans through convection currents.

subject: Science
description: Students know heat from Earth’s interior reaches the surface primarily through convection.
description_plain: Your child knows that heat from inside the Earth reaches the surface mainly by convection.

subject: Science
description: Students know solar energy reaches Earth through radiation, mostly in the form of visible light.
description_plain: Your child understands that sunlight reaches Earth mainly as visible light through radiation.

subject: Science
description: Students know the sun is the major source of energy for phenomena on Earth’s surface; it powers winds, ocean currents, and the water cycle.
description_plain: Your child knows the sun provides energy that powers winds, ocean currents, and the water cycle on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Energy in the Earth's System: Many phenomena on Earth’s surface are affected by the transfer of energy through radiation and convection currents. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that many things on Earth’s surface change because energy moves through radiation and moving currents.

subject: Science
description: Students know heat energy is also transferred between objects by radiation (radiation can travel through space).
description_plain: Your child knows heat can move between objects through radiation, which can travel through space.

subject: Science
description: Students know heat flows in solids by conduction (which involves no flow of matter) and in fluids by conduction and by convection (which involves flow of matter).
description_plain: Your child understands heat moves through solids by conduction and through fluids by conduction and convection, which involves movement of the fluid.

subject: Science
description: Students know that when fuel is consumed, most of the energy released becomes heat energy.
description_plain: Your child knows that when fuel burns, most of the energy released turns into heat.

subject: Science
description: Students know energy can be carried from one place to another by heat flow or by waves, including water, light and sound waves, or by moving objects.
description_plain: Your child understands energy can move from one place to another by heat, waves like water, light, and sound, or by moving objects.

subject: Science
description: Heat (Thermal Energy- Physical Sciences): Heat moves in a predictable flow from warmer objects to cooler objects until all the objects are at the same temperature. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child knows heat always moves from warmer things to cooler things until they reach the same temperature.

subject: Science
description: Students know earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, and floods change human and wildlife habitats.
description_plain: Your child understands that earthquakes, volcanoes, landslides, and floods can change where people and animals live.

subject: Science
description: Students know beaches are dynamic systems in which the sand is supplied by rivers and moved along the coast by the action of waves.
description_plain: Your child knows beaches change because rivers bring sand and waves move it along the shore.

subject: Science
description: Students know rivers and streams are dynamic systems that erode, transport sediment, change course, and flood their banks in natural and recurring patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands rivers and streams change by wearing away land, moving sediment, changing direction, and flooding in natural patterns.

subject: Science
description: Students know water running downhill is the dominant process in shaping the landscape, including California’s landscape.
description_plain: Your child knows that flowing water is the main force shaping the land, including in California.

subject: Science
description: Shaping the Earth's Surface: Topography is reshaped by the weathering of rock and soil and by the transportation and deposition of sediment. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that the shape of the Earth’s surface changes because rocks and soil break down and sediment moves and settles.

subject: Science
description: Students know how to determine the epicenter of an earthquake and know that the effects of an earthquake on any region vary, depending on the size of the earthquake, the distance of the region from the epicenter, the local geology, and the type of construction in the region.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find the center of an earthquake and understands that earthquake effects depend on its size, distance, ground type, and building styles.

subject: Science
description: Students know how to explain major features of California geology (including mountains, faults, volcanoes) in terms of plate tectonics.
description_plain: Your child can explain California’s mountains, faults, and volcanoes by understanding how tectonic plates move.

subject: Science
description: Students know major geologic events, such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and mountain building, result from plate motions.
description_plain: Your child knows that big events like earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain building happen because tectonic plates move.

subject: Science
description: Students know that earthquakes are sudden motions along breaks in the crust called faults and that volcanoes and fissures are locations where magma reaches the surface.
description_plain: Your child understands earthquakes happen when the Earth’s crust breaks and moves, and volcanoes are places where magma comes out.

subject: Science
description: Students know lithospheric plates the size of continents and oceans move at rates of centimeters per year in response to movements in the mantle.
description_plain: Your child knows that large plates under the Earth’s surface move slowly because of movements in the layer below them.

subject: Science
description: Students know Earth is composed of several layers: a cold, brittle lithosphere; a hot, convecting mantle; and a dense, metallic core.
description_plain: Your child understands Earth has layers: a cold, hard outer layer; a hot, moving middle layer; and a dense, metal core inside.

subject: Science
description: Students know evidence of plate tectonics is derived from the fit of the continents; the location of earthquakes, volcanoes, and midocean ridges; and the distribution of fossils, rock types, and ancient climatic zones.
description_plain: Your child knows we have proof that tectonic plates move by looking at how continents fit, where earthquakes and volcanoes happen, and where fossils and rocks are found.

subject: Science
description: Plate Tectonics and Earth Structure: Plate tectonics accounts for important features of Earth’s surface and major geologic events. As a basis for understanding this concept:
description_plain: Your child understands that moving tectonic plates explain many features on Earth’s surface and major geological events.

subject: Science
description: write a report of an investigation that includes conducting tests, collecting data or examining evidence, and drawing conclusions.
description_plain: Your child can write a report about an investigation by testing, collecting data, and drawing conclusions.

subject: Science
description: draw conclusions from scientific evidence and indicate whether further information is needed to support a specific conclusion.
description_plain: Your child can make conclusions from scientific evidence and decide if more information is needed.

subject: Science
description: record data by using appropriate graphic representations (including charts, graphs, and labeled diagrams) and make inferences based on those data.
description_plain: Your child can record data using charts, graphs, or diagrams and make guesses based on that data.

subject: Science
description: select appropriate tools (e.g., thermometers, meter sticks, balances, and graduated cylinders) and make quantitative observations.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right tools like thermometers or rulers to make measurements and observations.

subject: Science
description: identify a single independent variable in a scientific investigation and explain how this variable can be used to collect information to answer a question about the results of the experiment.
description_plain: Your child can identify one variable to change in an experiment and explain how it helps answer a question.

subject: Science
description: plan and conduct a simple investigation based on a student-developed question and write instructions others can follow to carry out the procedure.
description_plain: Your child can plan and do a simple experiment based on a question they create and write clear steps for others to follow.

subject: Science
description: develop a testable question.
description_plain: develop a testable question.

subject: Science
description: classify objects (e.g., rocks, plants, leaves) in accordance with appropriate criteria.
description_plain: Your child can group objects like rocks or plants based on shared features.

subject: Science
description: know the path of a planet around the Sun is due to the gravitational attraction between the Sun and the planet.
description_plain: Your child knows that a planet moves around the Sun because of the Sun’s gravity pulling on it.

subject: Science
description: know the solar system includes the planet Earth, the Moon, the Sun, eight other planets and their satellites, and smaller objects, such as asteroids and comets.
description_plain: Your child knows the solar system includes Earth, the Moon, the Sun, eight other planets with their moons, and smaller objects like asteroids and comets.

subject: Science
description: know the Sun, an average star, is the central and largest body in the solar system and is composed primarily of hydrogen and helium.
description_plain: Your child knows the Sun is the biggest object in the solar system and is mostly made of hydrogen and helium.

subject: Science
description: know that the Earth’s atmosphere exerts a pressure that decreases with distance above Earth’s surface and that at any point it exerts this pressure equally in all directions.
description_plain: Your child understands that air pressure from Earth’s atmosphere gets weaker as you go higher and pushes equally in all directions at any spot.

subject: Science
description: know how to use weather maps and data to predict local weather and know that weather forecasts depend on many variables.
description_plain: Your child can use weather maps and data to predict local weather and knows forecasts depend on many factors.

subject: Science
description: know the causes and effects of different types of severe weather.
description_plain: Your child knows what causes different severe weather and what effects they have.

subject: Science
description: know the influence that the ocean has on the weather and the role that the water cycle plays in weather patterns.
description_plain: Your child understands how the ocean affects weather and how the water cycle influences weather patterns.

subject: Science
description: know uneven heating of Earth causes air movements (convection currents).
description_plain: Your child knows uneven heating of Earth causes air to move in currents.

subject: Science
description: know the origin of the water used by their local communities.
description_plain: Your child knows where the water used by their local community comes from.

subject: Science
description: know that the amount of fresh water located in rivers, lakes, underground sources, and glaciers is limited and that its availability can be extended by recycling and decreasing the use of water.
description_plain: Your child understands that fresh water in rivers, lakes, underground, and glaciers is limited and can last longer by recycling and using less.

subject: Science
description: know water vapor in the air moves from one place to another and can form fog or clouds, which are tiny droplets of water or ice, and can fall to Earth as rain, hail, sleet, or snow.
description_plain: Your child knows water vapor moves through the air, forms fog or clouds, and falls as rain, hail, sleet, or snow.

subject: Science
description: know when liquid water evaporates, it turns into water vapor in the air and can reappear as a liquid when cooled or as a solid if cooled below the freezing point of water.
description_plain: Your child understands that when water evaporates, it becomes vapor in the air and can turn back into liquid or ice when cooled.

subject: Science
description: know most of Earth’s water is present as salt water in the oceans, which cover most of Earth’s surface.
description_plain: Your child understands that most of Earth's water is salty and found in the oceans, which cover most of the planet's surface.

subject: Science
description: know plant and animal cells break down sugar to obtain energy, a process resulting in carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (respiration).
description_plain: Your child knows that plant and animal cells break down sugar to get energy, producing carbon dioxide and water in the process.

subject: Science
description: know plants use carbon dioxide (CO2) and energy from sunlight to build molecules of sugar and release oxygen.
description_plain: Your child understands that plants use carbon dioxide and sunlight to make sugar and release oxygen.

subject: Science
description: know how sugar, water, and minerals are transported in a vascular plant.
description_plain: Your child knows how sugar, water, and minerals move through a plant's transport system.

subject: Science
description: know the role of the kidney in removing cellular waste from blood and converting it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.
description_plain: Your child understands that kidneys remove waste from the blood and turn it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.

subject: Science
description: know the sequential steps of digestion and the roles of teeth and the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and colon in the function of the digestive system.
description_plain: Your child knows the steps of digestion and how the mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and colon each help break down food.

subject: Science
description: know how blood circulates through the heart chambers, lungs, and body and how carbon dioxide (CO2) and oxygen (O2) are exchanged in the lungs and tissues.
description_plain: Your child understands how blood moves through the heart, lungs, and body, and how oxygen and carbon dioxide are exchanged in the lungs and tissues.

subject: Science
description: know many multi-cellular organisms have specialized structures to support the transport of materials.
description_plain: Your child knows that many living things have special parts that help move materials around their bodies.

subject: Science
description: know the common properties of salts, such as sodium chloride (NaCl).
description_plain: Your child knows common traits of salts, like table salt (sodium chloride).

subject: Science
description: know living organisms and most materials are composed of just a few elements
description_plain: Your child understands that living things and most materials are made from just a few basic elements.

subject: Science
description: know properties of solid, liquid, and gaseous substances, such as sugar (C 6 H 12 O 6 ), w ater ( H 2 O), helium (He), oxygen ( O 2 ), nitrogen (N 2 ), and carbon dioxide (C O 2 ).
description_plain: Your child knows the differences between solids, liquids, and gases using examples like sugar, water, helium, oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon dioxide.

subject: Science
description: know differences in chemical and physical properties of substances are used to separate mixtures and identify compounds.
description_plain: Your child understands that differences in how substances behave help us separate mixtures and identify compounds.

subject: Science
description: know scientists have developed instruments that can create discrete i m ages of atoms and molecules that show that the atoms and molecules often occur in well - ordered arrays.
description_plain: Your child knows that scientists use special tools to see atoms and molecules arranged in patterns.

subject: Science
description: know that each element is made of one kind of atom and that the elements are organized in the periodic table by their chemical properties.
description_plain: Your child understands that each element is made of one kind of atom and that elements are organized by their properties in the periodic table.

subject: Science
description: know metals have properties in common, such as high electrical and thermal conductivity. Some metals, such as aluminum (Al), iron (Fe), nickel (Ni), copper (Cu), silver (Ag), and gold (Au), are pure elements; others, such as steel and brass, are composed of a combination of elemental metals.
description_plain: Your child knows that metals share traits like conducting electricity and heat, and that some metals are pure while others are mixtures.

subject: Science
description: know all matter is made of atoms, which may combine to form molecules.
description_plain: Your child understands that all matter is made of atoms, which can join together to form molecules.

subject: Science
description: know that during chemical reactions the atoms in the reactants rearrange to form products with different properties .
description_plain: Your child knows that during chemical reactions, atoms rearrange to make new substances with different properties.

subject: Science
description: Physical Science: Elements and their combinations account for all the varied types of matter in the world. As a basis for understanding this concept, students will:
description_plain: Your child understands that all matter is made from elements and their combinations.

description_plain: Your child understands that systems are groups of parts that work together, and models help us study these systems.

description_plain: Your child understands how size, amount, and scale affect how things work and relate to each other.

subject: Science
description: Humans depend on the living world for the resources and other benefits provided by biodiversity. But human activity is also having adverse impacts on biodiversity through overpopulation, overexploitation, habitat destruction, pollution, introduction of invasive species, and climate change. Thus sustaining biodiversity so that ecosystem functioning and productivity are maintained is essential to supporting and enhancing life on Earth. Sustaining biodiversity also aids humanity by preserving landscapes of recreational or inspirational value.
description_plain: Your child knows that humans rely on biodiversity for resources and benefits, but human actions can harm it, so protecting biodiversity is important for life and enjoyment on Earth.

subject: Science
description: Species become extinct because they can no longer survive and reproduce in their altered environment. If members cannot adjust to change that is too fast or drastic, the opportunity for the species' evolution is lost.
description_plain: Your child understands that species go extinct when they can't survive or reproduce in changed environments, especially if changes happen too fast.

description_plain: Your child understands that species go extinct when they can't survive or reproduce in changed environments, especially if changes happen too fast.

subject: Science
description: The traits that positively affect survival are more likely to be reproduced, and thus are more common in the population.
description_plain: Your child knows that traits helping survival are more likely to be passed on and become common in a population.

subject: Science
description: The traits that positively affect survival are more likely to be reproduced, and thus are more common in the population.
description_plain: Your child knows that traits helping survival are more likely to be passed on and become common in a population.

description: The traits that positively affect survival are more likely to be reproduced, and thus are more common in the population.
description_plain: Your child knows that traits helping survival are more likely to be passed on and become common in a population.

description: The traits that positively affect survival are more likely to be reproduced, and thus are more common in the population.
description_plain: Your child knows that traits helping survival are more likely to be passed on and become common in a population.

subject: Science
description: Genetic information provides evidence of evolution. DNA sequences vary among species, but there are many overlaps; in fact, the ongoing branching that produces multiple lines of descent can be inferred by comparing the DNA sequences of different organisms. Such information is also derivable from the similarities and differences in amino acid sequences and from anatomical and embryological evidence.
description_plain: Your child understands that DNA shows how species are related and have evolved by comparing similarities and differences.

subject: Science
description: Environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. Thus the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.
description_plain: Your child knows that both genes and the environment affect how traits appear and how common they are in a group.

description_plain: Your child knows that both genes and the environment affect how traits appear and how common they are in a group.

subject: Science
description: Environmental factors also affect expression of traits, and hence affect the probability of occurrences of traits in a population. Thus the variation and distribution of traits observed depends on both genetic and environmental factors.
description_plain: Your child knows that both genes and the environment affect how traits appear and how common they are in a group.

subject: Science
description: Each chromosome consists of a single very long DNA molecule, and each gene on the chromosome is a particular segment of that DNA. The instructions for forming species' characteristics are carried in DNA. All cells in an organism have the same genetic content, but the genes used (expressed) by the cell may be regulated in different ways. Not all DNA codes for a protein; some segments of DNA are involved in regulatory or structural functions, and some have no as-yet known function.
description_plain: Your child understands that chromosomes carry DNA with instructions for traits, and cells use different genes in different ways.

description_plain: Your child understands that chromosomes carry DNA with instructions for traits, and cells use different genes in different ways.

subject: Science
description: Ecosystems have carrying capacities, which are limits to the numbers of organisms and populations they can support. These limits result from such factors as the availability of living and nonliving resources and from such challenges such as predation, competition, and disease. Organisms would have the capacity to produce populations of great size were it not for the fact that environments and resources are finite. This fundamental tension affects the abundance (number of individuals) of species in any given ecosystem.
description_plain: Your child understands that ecosystems can only support a limited number of organisms because resources and space are limited.

subject: Science
description: As a result of these chemical reactions, energy is transferred from one system of interacting molecules to another. Cellular respiration is a chemical process in which the bonds of food molecules and oxygen molecules are broken and new compounds are formed that can transport energy to muscles. Cellular respiration also releases the energy needed to maintain body temperature despite ongoing energy transfer to the surrounding environment.
description_plain: Your child knows that cellular respiration breaks down food and oxygen to release energy needed for body functions like keeping warm.

subject: Science
description: In multicellular organisms individual cells grow and then divide via a process called mitosis, thereby allowing the organism to grow. The organism begins as a single cell (fertilized egg) that divides successively to produce many cells, with each parent cell passing identical genetic material (two variants of each chromosome pair) to both daughter cells. Cellular division and differentiation produce and maintain a complex organism, composed of systems of tissues and organs that work together to meet the needs of the whole organism.
description_plain: Your child understands that organisms grow as their cells divide and specialize to form tissues and organs that work together.

subject: Math
description: Fit a linear function for a scatter plot that suggests a linear association.
description_plain: Your child can draw a straight line that best fits a scatter plot when the points show a linear pattern.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the fit of a function by plotting and analyzing residuals.
description_plain: Your child can check how well a function fits data by looking at graphs of the differences between the data and the function.

subject: Math
description: Fit a function to the data; use functions fitted to data to solve problems in the context of the data. Use given functions or choose a function suggested by the context. Emphasize linear, quadratic, and exponential models.
description_plain: Your child can choose or use a function that fits data and use it to solve problems, focusing on linear, quadratic, and exponential types.

subject: Math
description: Recognize situations in which a quantity grows or decays by a constant percent rate per unit interval relative to another.
description_plain: Your child can recognize when something grows or shrinks by the same percent over equal time periods.

subject: Math
description: Recognize situations in which one quantity changes at a constant rate per unit interval relative to another.
description_plain: Your child can recognize when one quantity changes by the same amount over equal time periods.

subject: Math
description: Prove that linear functions grow by equal differences over equal intervals, and that exponential functions grow by equal factors over equal intervals.
description_plain: Your child can show that linear functions increase by the same amount over equal intervals, and exponential functions increase by the same factor over equal intervals.

subject: Math
description: Solve an equation of the form f(x) = c for a simple function f that has an inverse and write an expression for the inverse.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple equations where a function equals a number and write the expression for the inverse function.

subject: Math
description: Combine standard function types using arithmetic operations. For example, build a function that models the temperature of a cooling body by adding a constant function to a decaying exponential, and relate these functions to the model.
description_plain: Your child can combine different types of functions by adding, subtracting, multiplying, or dividing them to model situations, like how something cools down.

subject: Math
description: Determine an explicit expression, a recursive process, or steps for calculation from a context.
description_plain: Your child can write a clear rule or steps to find values based on a real-life situation.

subject: Math
description: Use the properties of exponents to interpret expressions for exponential functions. For example, identify percent rate of change in functions such as y = (1.02)^t , y = (0.97)^t , y = (1.01)^12t, and y = (1.2)^(t/10), and classify them as representing exponential growth or decay.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about exponents to understand exponential functions and tell if they show growth or decay.

subject: Math
description: Use the process of factoring and completing the square in a quadratic function to show zeros, extreme values, and symmetry of the graph, and interpret these in terms of a context.
description_plain: Your child can use factoring and completing the square to find where a quadratic function crosses the axis, its highest or lowest points, and its symmetry, and explain what these mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Graph exponential and logarithmic functions, showing intercepts and end behavior, and trigonometric functions, showing period, midline, and amplitude.
description_plain: Your child can draw graphs of exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions, showing key points like intercepts and how the graph behaves over time, including the period, midline, and height of waves.

subject: Math
description: Graph square root, cube root, and piecewise-defined functions, including step functions and absolute value functions.
description_plain: Your child can draw graphs of square root, cube root, step, and absolute value functions, including those made up of different pieces.

subject: Math
description: Graph linear and quadratic functions and show intercepts, maxima, and minima.
description_plain: Your child can draw graphs of straight lines and parabolas, showing where they cross the axes and their highest or lowest points.

subject: Math
description: Solve quadratic equations by inspection (e.g., for x2 = 49), taking square roots, completing the square, the quadratic formula, and factoring, as appropriate to the initial form of the equation. Recognize when the quadratic formula gives complex solutions and write them as a ± bi for real numbers a and b.
description_plain: Your child can solve quadratic equations using different methods like taking square roots, factoring, completing the square, or the quadratic formula, and can write complex solutions when needed.

subject: Math
description: Use the method of completing the square to transform any quadratic equation in x into an equation of the form (x – p)2 = q that has the same solutions. Derive the quadratic formula from this form.
description_plain: Your child can change any quadratic equation into a form that makes it easy to find solutions by completing the square and can use this to understand where the quadratic formula comes from.

subject: Math
description: Use the properties of exponents to transform expressions for exponential functions. For example, the expression 1.15t can be rewritten as (1.15^(1/12))^12t ≈ 1.01212t to reveal the approximate equivalent monthly interest rate if the annual rate is 15%.
description_plain: Your child can use rules about exponents to rewrite exponential expressions in different ways, like showing how an annual interest rate breaks down into monthly rates.

subject: Math
description: Complete the square in a quadratic expression to reveal the maximum or minimum value of the function it defines.
description_plain: Your child can complete the square in a quadratic expression to find its highest or lowest value.

subject: Math
description: Factor a quadratic expression to reveal the zeros of the function it defines.
description_plain: Your child can factor quadratic expressions to find where the function equals zero.

subject: Math
description: Interpret complicated expression by viewing one or more of their parts as a single entity. For example, interpret P(1+r)^n as the product of P and a factor not dependent on r.
description_plain: Your child can understand complicated expressions by looking at parts of them as single units, like seeing P(1+r)^n as P times a factor.

subject: Math
description: Interpret parts of an expression, such as terms, factors and coefficients.
description_plain: Your child can explain parts of an expression, such as terms, factors, and numbers that multiply.

subject: Math
description: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.7.NS.A.1.D Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to add and subtract fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.7.NS.A.1.C Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p - q = p + (-q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands that subtracting a number is the same as adding its opposite, and can use this to find distances between numbers on a number line in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.7.NS.A.1.B Understand p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative. Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (are additive inverses). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child understands adding numbers means moving left or right on a number line depending on if the number is positive or negative, and knows that a number plus its opposite equals zero.

subject: Math
description: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.7.NS.A.1.A Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. For example, a hydrogen atom has 0 charge because its two constituents are oppositely charged.
description_plain: Your child can explain situations where opposite amounts cancel each other out to make zero, like how a hydrogen atom has no charge.

subject: Math
description: CCSS.MATH.CONTENT.7.NS.A.1 Apply and extend previous understandings of addition and subtraction to add and subtract rational numbers; represent addition and subtraction on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers and show these operations on a number line going left-right or up-down.

subject: Social Studies
